intro.title: if elif else
intro.bodyMarkdown:
Conditional statements let your program choose different paths. `if condition:` runs a block when the condition is true; `elif` checks another condition; `else` runs when none matched.

Indentation defines which lines belong to each branch. Conditions are expressions that evaluate to True or False.

Use if/elif/else to handle user choices, validate data, and implement game rules.

challenges.title: Easy: else branch
Medium: elif chain
Expert: Truthy check
challenges.promptMarkdown: If lst: print `has items` for lst=[1].

challenges.title: Expert: if not
challenges.promptMarkdown: Print `empty` when s="" using if not.

questions.prompt: Keyword for additional condition after if?

intro.title: Boolean Logic
intro.bodyMarkdown:
Booleans represent truth: `True` and `False`. Combine conditions with `and`, `or`, and `not`. Short-circuit evaluation skips right side when result is already determined.

Comparison operators produce booleans. Membership `in` and identity `is` also return True/False.

Clear boolean expressions make conditions readable; avoid overly nested logic when elif chains are clearer.
intro.sandboxCode:
a, b = True, False
print(a and b, a or b, not a)
print(3 in [1,2,3])
print(None is None)

intro.title: Comparison Operators
intro.bodyMarkdown:
Compare values with `==`, `!=`, `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=`. They return True or False without modifying operands.

Chained comparisons like `1 < x < 10` are idiomatic Python. Strings compare lexicographically; types must usually be compatible.

Use comparisons in if statements, while loops, and sorting keys.

intro.title: while Loops
intro.bodyMarkdown:
A `while condition:` loop repeats its body while the condition is true. Update variables inside the loop to avoid infinite loops.

while suits unknown iteration counts — reading until sentinel, retrying input, or game loops.

Always ensure the condition eventually becomes false, or use break to exit early.
intro.sandboxCode:
n = 3
while n > 0:
    print(n)
    n -= 1
print("done")

intro.title: for Loops
intro.bodyMarkdown:
`for item in iterable:` visits each element in a sequence or collection. It is the preferred loop when you know what you are iterating over.

Loop over strings, lists, ranges, dict keys, and file lines. The loop variable takes each value in turn.

for loops are clearer than while for most collection processing and avoid manual index management.

intro.title: range()
intro.bodyMarkdown:
`range(stop)` produces 0..stop-1. `range(start, stop)` and `range(start, stop, step)` control bounds and stride.

range objects are lazy sequences — memory efficient for large spans. Convert with `list(range(5))` to see values.

range is the standard partner to for loops for numeric iteration.
intro.sandboxCode:
print(list(range(5)))
print(list(range(2, 8, 2)))
for i in range(3):
    print(i ** 2)

challenges.title: Expert: Index loop

intro.title: break and continue
intro.bodyMarkdown:
`break` exits the innermost loop immediately. `continue` skips the rest of the current iteration and jumps to the next.

Use break when you found what you need; use continue to skip invalid items without nesting deep if blocks.

break and continue apply to the nearest enclosing loop only, not outer nested loops.

challenges.title: Easy: break early
challenges.promptMarkdown: Loop 0..9 break at 3; last printed 2 or count iterations 3.
challenges.starterCode:
c=0
for i in range(10):
 c+=1
 if i==3: break

intro.title: Nested Loops
intro.bodyMarkdown:
Place one loop inside another to process grids, pairs, or combinations. Inner loop runs completely for each outer iteration.

Common for multiplication tables, 2D lists, and comparing all pairs. Watch complexity — double loops over large data can be slow.

Break inner loop only exits inner; use flags or functions to break outer from inner when needed.

intro.title: match case
intro.bodyMarkdown:
Python 3.10+ adds `match value:` with `case pattern:` branches — structural pattern matching similar to switch in other languages but more powerful.

Match literals, capture variables, use `|` for alternatives, and `case _:` as wildcard default.

Use match when you have many discrete cases on one value; if/elif remains fine for simple conditions.
intro.sandboxCode:
def http_status(n):
    match n:
        case 200: return "OK"
        case 404: return "Not Found"
        case _: return "Unknown"
print(http_status(404))

 case x if x>0:
  pos=True

intro.title: Loop Patterns and Algorithms
intro.bodyMarkdown:
Common patterns: accumulate (sum/product), search (find first match), filter (build new list), and map (transform each item).

Recognizing patterns helps you pick for vs while and avoid bugs. Many algorithms are loops with careful state updates.

Practice counting, finding max/min, and early exit — foundations for later data structures and projects.

Medium: Filter positives
Hard: First index
Expert: Running average

s=0
for n in nums: s+=n
avg=s/len(nums)

levels.title: Easy project: CLI guess the number

intro.title: Easy project: CLI guess the number
intro.bodyMarkdown:
Build a number-guessing game in the terminal. Pick a secret, loop until the player guesses correctly, give higher/lower hints.

Use `random.randint`, a while loop, and int conversion with try/except for bad input.

Structure with a `main()` function and `if __name__ == "__main__"` guard.

challenges.title: Medium: higher lower

intro.title: Medium project: Quiz app
intro.bodyMarkdown:
Questions as list of dicts with prompt, choices, answer index. Score correct answers.

Shuffle questions or choices with random.shuffle. Show final percentage.

Time optional; store high score in file.
intro.sandboxCode:
questions = [
    {"q": "2+2?", "choices": ["3","4","5"], "a": 1},
]

def grade(answers):
    score = sum(1 for i, ans in enumerate(answers) if ans == questions[i]["a"])
    return score

print(grade([1]))

validation.setupCode:
questions=[{"a":1},{"a":0}]
answers=[1,0]
score=sum(1 for i,a in enumerate(answers) if a==questions[i]["a"]

intro.title: Hard project: FizzBuzz mini
intro.bodyMarkdown:
Print numbers 1–15, but replace multiples of 3 with `Fizz`, multiples of 5 with `Buzz`, and multiples of both with `FizzBuzz`.

Use a for loop and if/elif branches. Collect results in a list, then print the list.
